Calcification focus: Deposits of calcium salts; purple-blue, structureless granules or masses.
Smooth muscle cell: Spindle-shaped cells with uniform cytoplasm arranged in parallel.
T cell: Small round nucleus, scant cytoplasm, involved in cellular immunity, often diffusely distributed.
Lymph node tissue: Contains lymphoid follicles and medullary cords with distinct zones.
Tumor necrosis area: Loss of cellular structure and pale staining, possibly accompanied by hemorrhage or calcification.
Capillary: Smallest blood vessels, usually formed by a single layer of endothelial cells.
Papillary structures: Finger-like epithelial proliferations protruding into glandular lumens.
Proximal tubule: Strong reabsorption function, abundant cytoplasm, with a prominent brush border.
Stromal collagen deposition: Collagen protein accumulation, commonly seen in scar or fibrotic areas.
Nuclear division: The morphology of the cell nucleus in the division phase indicates the activity of cell proliferation.
B cell: Morphologically similar to T cells, often aggregated to form follicles.
Inflammatory cell infiltration: Degree of immune cells (e.g., lymphocytes, macrophages) invading tumor areas, indicating immune response status.
Cystic change: Formation of cystic spaces within tumor tissue.
Hemorrhage area: Extensive red blood cell extravasation, appearing as red-stained patches.
Fibroblast: Stromal cell with spindle-shaped nucleus, involved in collagen synthesis.
Atypical tumor cells: Cells with markedly abnormal morphology; enlarged, hyperchromatic, irregularly shaped nuclei, indicating high malignancy.
Mitotic figures: Cells undergoing mitosis, indicating active proliferation.
Lymphoid follicle: Spherical structure formed by B cell aggregation; central zone often lighter.
Neutrophil: Multilobed nucleus; abundant infiltration during acute inflammation.
Adipose tissue: Large vacuolated cytoplasm with nuclei pushed to the periphery.
Distal tubule: Regular lumen, scant cytoplasm, cells tightly arranged.
Endothelial cell: Flat cells lining blood vessels, surrounding the vascular lumen.
Tumor-associated macrophage (TAM): Large cytoplasm, distributed in tumor stroma and necrotic areas.
Dendritic cell: Cells with branching processes; antigen-presenting function, easily identified by immunostaining.
Mast cell: Cytoplasm contains coarse granules; difficult to identify on H&E.
Basement membrane: Boundary between epithelium and stroma; not distinct in H&E staining.
Medullary region: Inner kidney region mainly composed of Henle’s loop and collecting ducts.
Collecting duct: Large lumen, cuboidal or columnar cells, predominantly in the medulla.
Cortical region: Outer layer of the kidney containing glomeruli and numerous tubules.
Lymphatic vessel: Thin wall, large lumen, content sparse or empty.
Mucinous change: Presence of mucin-like stroma in tumor tissue.
Glomerulus: The filtration unit of the nephron; spherical with densely packed central capillaries.
Small artery: Has a smooth muscle layer; thicker wall and smaller lumen.
Cytoplasmic transparency: Cytoplasmic clearing is often observed in certain renal cell carcinomas.
Nerve fiber: Wavy, lightly stained structures, usually requiring special stains for identification.
Clear tubule structures: Tumor cell structures forming clear, tubular arrangements.
Bowman’s capsule: Double-layered sac enclosing the glomerulus, forming the urinary space.
Henle’s loop: Site of urine concentration; appears as elongated tubules in cross-section.
Renal capsule: Dense connective tissue outer layer of the kidney; pink, band-like structure.
Angiogenesis pattern: Density and distribution pattern of new blood vessels, affecting tumor growth and invasion.
Tumor cells: Renal epithelial cells that have undergone malignant transformation; disorganized structure, cytoplasm is clear or eosinophilic, with nuclear atypia.
Myofibroblast: Activated fibroblast with contractile function, abundant cytoplasm.
Plasma cell: Eccentric nucleus with perinuclear halo; mature B cell secreting antibodies.
Foamy macrophage: Macrophages containing lipid droplets in the cytoplasm.
Tumor nucleoli: Highly active nuclear structures, large and prominent, often used for grading.
Tumor-associated blood vessels: Newly formed vessels, often irregular in shape with thin, fragile walls.
